revision.comment: Bot: mark 1 deleted QID(s) as DELETED_QID in ill templates [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/24608848913|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:氷室権現]] {{Infobox deity|venerated_in={{ill|Himuro Shrine|qid=Q11549570|1=Himuro Shrine}}|equivalent5_type=Japanese |equivalent5={{ill|Tsuge no Ōyamanushi|ja|闘鶏大山主|1=Tsuge no Ōyamanushi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuge no Ōyamanushi|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuge no Ōyamanushi|qq=}}}}
happened within Ice Houses (Himuro). A bit confusing as to why they did them there of all places but gotta work on finding more sources to make all of this clear. Increasing support for conflating {{ill|Tsuge no Ōyamanushi|ja|闘鶏大山主|1=Tsuge no Ōyamanushi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuge no Ōyamanushi|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuge no Ōyamanushi|qq=}} and {{ill|Himuro Gongen|ja|氷室権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himuro Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 氷室}} but still no hard proof[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 23:58, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} . My gut says yes but see no sources[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:19, 8 March 2025 (UTC)}} '''Himuro Gongen''' (氷室権現) is a syncretic deity of Shinto and Buddhism, enshrined in various places across Japan in connection with {{ill|Ice house (building)|en|Ice house (building)|qid=Q253893|lt=ice houses|1=Ice house (building)}} known as Himuro in Japan, which are facilities for storing natural ice. The term "Gongen" (権現) refers to a Buddha appearing in the temporary form of a Shinto deity, and Himuro Gongen is deeply associated with the faith concerning the preservation and use of ice.
The {{ill|Himuro Shrine|en|Himuro Shrine|qid=Q11549570|lt=Himuro Shrine|1=Himuro Shrine}} in {{ill|Kasugano, Nara|ja|春日野町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasugano, Nara|1=Kasugano, Nara City|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q18988212}}, Nara City, Nara Prefecture, was established after the relocation of the capital to Heian-kyō to enshrine the himuro of Nara and is also known as Himuro Gongen.<ref>https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%B0%B7%E5%AE%A4</ref>
This shrine has been worshiped as a deity overseeing the preservation and supply of ice.
Additionally, at the summit of {{ill|Mount Himuro|ja|氷室岳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Himuro|1=Mount Himuro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11549563}} (elevation 562.6 meters), located on the border between {{ill|Yanai, Yamaguchi|en|Yanai, Yamaguchi|qid=Q819678|lt=Yanai, Yamaguchi|1=Yanai, Yamaguchi}} and {{ill|Iwakuni|en|Iwakuni|qid=Q329797|lt=Iwakuni, Yamaguchi|1=Iwakuni}} in Yamaguchi Prefecture, there is a small shrine dedicated to Himuro Gongen.<ref>https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%B0%B7%E5%AE%A4%E5%B2%B3</ref>
This mountain has long been an object of mountain worship and is locally known as "Suō Fuji" (周防富士) or "Dakeyama" (嶽山).
Furthermore, within the grounds of {{ill|Hattasan Soneiji|ja|法多山尊永寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hattasan Soneiji|1=Son'ei-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11555090}} in {{ill|Fukuroi, Shizuoka|en|Fukuroi, Shizuoka|qid=Q821337|lt=Fukuroi, Shizuoka|1=Fukuroi, Shizuoka}}, Shizuoka Prefecture, there is another Himuro Shrine, which has been worshiped since ancient times as a deity for healing illnesses.<ref>https://www.hattasan.or.jp/annai/12himurojinja/</ref>
At this shrine, the "Kenpyō-shiki" (献氷式, Ice Offering Ceremony) is held annually on July 1st, where ice is dedicated to pray for relief from the summer heat and for good health.
The faith in Himuro Gongen originates from a time when ice preservation technology was highly valuable, and people revered the blessings of ice as sacred, offering gratitude and prayers. Even today, this tradition continues through various Himuro Shrines and related festivals across Japan.
== Himuro in Japan ==
In Japan, during times when there was no technology for {{ill|Icemaker|en|Icemaker|qid=Q4271166|lt=artificial ice production|1=Icemaker}} from spring to autumn, it was necessary to store natural ice formed in winter to prevent it from melting. Although there are no precise records, it is believed that caves or holes dug in the ground were covered with huts made of {{ill|Thatching|en|Thatching|qid=Q874531|lt=thatch|1=Thatching}} and other materials to keep the ice cool. The inside of the Himuro remained cooler than the outside air due to the {{ill|evaporative cooling|ja|気化熱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/evaporative cooling|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 蒸発熱|1=Enthalpy of vaporization|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q483538}} of {{ill|Groundwater|en|Groundwater|qid=Q161598|lt=groundwater|1=Groundwater}}, allowing ice to be preserved until summer, especially in cool mountain areas. In such times, when only natural ice could be stored, summer ice was a precious commodity, long reserved for the {{ill|Royal court|en|Royal court|qid=Q1134237|lt=Imperial Court|1=Royal court}} and the {{ill|shogunate|ja|将軍家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shogunate|1=shogun family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11458630}}.
Historically, the first recorded mention appears in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, in the 62nd year of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|en|Emperor Nintoku|qid=Q313119|lt=Emperor Nintoku|1=Emperor Nintoku}}’s reign, describing how {{ill|Prince Nukata no Ōnakatsuhiko|ja|額田大中彦皇子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Nukata no Ōnakatsuhiko|1=Nukata no Ónakachuhiko no Miko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q55177820}} discovered something shining while hunting in {{ill|Tsuge|ja|闘鶏国造|1=Tsuge no Kuni no Miyatsuko|2=ja|3=闘鶏国造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuge|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuge no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}} (modern-day {{ill|Fukuzumi, Tenri City|ja|福住町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuzumi, Tenri City|1=fukusumichou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q16767272}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}). {{ill|Mokkan|en|Mokkan|qid=Q4299994|lt=Wooden tablets|1=Mokkan}} excavated from the site of {{ill|Prince Nagaya|en|Prince Nagaya|qid=Q1963492|lt=Prince Nagaya|1=Prince Nagaya}}’s residence from the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} also bear the inscription "Tsuge Himuro" (都祁氷室).<ref group="note">Excavated in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara|qq=}}.</ref>
In the Nihon Shoki, during the chronicle of {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|en|Emperor Kōtoku|qid=Q317980|lt=Emperor Kōtoku|1=Emperor Kōtoku}}, the surname {{ill|Muraji|en|Muraji|qid=Q2735611|lt=Murai|1=Muraji}} appears, suggesting the existence of officials responsible for managing the Himuro for the Imperial Court. For example, in the {{ill|Kamo clan|en|Kamo clan|qid=Q2975287|lt=Kamo clan|1=Kamo clan}}—a family that produced priests for {{ill|Kamo shrines|en|Kamo shrines|qid=Q1306606|lt=Kamo Shrine|1=Kamo shrines}}—genealogical records mention "Himuro" and the "Murai" surname.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|qid=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō}}, Himuro and ice production officials belonged to the {{ill|Shūmōdōshi|ja|主水司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūmōdōshi|1=Main Water Office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11368771}} of the {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|en|Ministry of the Imperial Household|qid=Q8193428|lt=Ministry of the Imperial Household|1=Ministry of the Imperial Household}}. The role of managing Himuro continued in various forms throughout history but disappeared during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}}.
In the {{ill|Hokuriku region|en|Hokuriku region|qid=Q381025|lt=Hokuriku region|1=Hokuriku region}}, from the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, there was a custom of presenting Himuro ice to the shogunate on {{ill|June 1 (Lunisolar)|ja|6月1日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 1 (Lunisolar)|qq=}} each year. However, with the Meiji period, natural ice became commercially available, and demand for refrigeration grew through the {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō period|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大正|1=Taishō era|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q329174}}, leading to the construction of new snow storage facilities. Conversely, from the 1890s onward, mechanical ice began replacing natural ice, and freezing facilities were developed. By the early {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa period|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 昭和|1=Shōwa era|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q327567}}, snow storage facilities were gradually phased out. After World War II, stricter regulations under the {{ill|Food Sanitation Act|ja|食品衛生法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Food Sanitation Act|1=Food Sanitation Act|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11666046}} and the widespread adoption of electric refrigerators in the 1960s led to the near-total disappearance of snow storage facilities by around 1962.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Ikegami|first=Yoshiyori|date=1999-04-28|title=The Distribution and Rise and Fall of Snow Storage Facilities in Hokuriku|url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/chirikagaku/54/2/54_KJ00003720149/_article/-char/ja/|journal=Geographical Science|volume=54|issue=2|pages=126, 129-134|publisher=Geographical Science Society|doi=10.20630/chirikagaku.54.2_126}}</ref>
There is also a {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|qid=Q226887|lt=Noh play|1=Noh}} with Himuro as its subject, classified as an aragami-mono (荒神物) play.
=== Himuro in the Engishiki ===
In the mid-{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|qid=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}} lists ten Himuro sites,<ref>[{{NDLDC|991103/539}} National History Series, Vol. 13] (Keizai Zasshi-sha, 1897–1901, National Diet Library Digital Collection) Frame 539.</ref><ref name="kokushi">"Himuro" entry in the {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=National History Encyclopedia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q6426904}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan).</ref> all under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Shūmōdōshi|ja|主水司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūmōdōshi|1=Main Water Office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11368771}} of the {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|en|Ministry of the Imperial Household|qid=Q8193428|lt=Ministry of the Imperial Household|1=Ministry of the Imperial Household}}:<ref name="kokushi"/>
* {{ill|Tsuge Himuro|ja|都介氷室|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuge Himuro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135578924|1=Tsuge Himuro}} ({{ill|Cockfighting|en|Cockfighting|qid=Q319771|lt=Tsuge|1=Cockfighting}}, now {{ill|Fukuzumi, Tenri City|ja|福住町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuzumi, Tenri City|1=fukusumichou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q16767272}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}) — Same as the "Tsuge Himuro" in Nihon Shoki. * {{ill|Tokuoka Himuro|ja|徳岡氷室|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokuoka Himuro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135578926|1=Tokuoka Himuro}} ({{ill|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q580186|lt=Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|1=Ukyō-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto City|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}) * {{ill|Ono Himuro|ja|小野氷室|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono Himuro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135578929|1=Ono Himuro}} (Himuroyama, Kamitakano, Sakyo-ku, Kyoto) * {{ill|Kurusuno Himuro|ja|栗栖野氷室|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurusuno Himuro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135578932|1=Kurusuno Himuro}} (Nishigamo Himurocho, Kita-ku, Kyoto) → {{ill|Himuro (Nishigamo, Kyoto)|ja|氷室 (京都市北区西賀茂)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himuro (Nishigamo, Kyoto)|1=Himuro Shrine (Kyoto)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11549558}} * {{ill|Tsuchisaka Himuro|ja|土坂氷室|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchisaka Himuro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135578934|1=Tsuchisaka Himuro}} (Nishi-Himurocho, Kita-ku, Kyoto) → {{ill|Himuro (Nishigamo, Kyoto)|ja|氷室 (京都市北区西賀茂)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himuro (Nishigamo, Kyoto)|1=Himuro Shrine (Kyoto)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11549558}} * {{ill|Sakakibara Himuro|ja|賢木原氷室|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakakibara Himuro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135578937|1=Sakakibara Himuro}} (Katsurabara, Nishikyo-ku, Kyoto) * {{ill|Iwasaki Himuro|ja|石前氷室|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwasaki Himuro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135578940|1=Iwasaki Himuro}} (Kinugasa Himurocho, Kita-ku, Kyoto) * {{ill|Sanra Himuro|ja|讃良氷室|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanra Himuro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135578943|1=Sanra Himuro}} (Muroike, Minamino, Shijonawate City, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|qid=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}) * {{ill|Buka Himuro|ja|部花氷室|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buka Himuro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135578945|1=Buka Himuro}} (Upper and Lower Ryuge Towns, Otsu City, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|qid=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture|1=Shiga Prefecture}}) * {{ill|Ikebe Himuro|ja|池辺氷室|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikebe Himuro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135578949|1=Ikebe Himuro}} (Kanki, Yagi Town, Nantan City, Kyoto) → {{ill|Hatahisa Shrine|ja|幡日佐神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatahisa Shrine|qq=}}
In {{ill|831|en|831|qid=Q30372|lt=831|1=831}} (Tenchō 8), three additional Himuro were established in both Yamashiro and Kawachi provinces.<ref name="kokushi"/><ref>"Nihon Kōki" fragments (found in "Nihon Kiraku"), entry dated August 20, 831.</ref>
=== Shrines Dedicated to Himuro ===
In {{ill|Heijō-kyō|en|Heijō-kyō|qid=Q862509|lt=Heijō-kyō|1=Heijō-kyō}}, a Himuro was placed on {{ill|Kasugayama|en|Kasugayama|qid=Q11513600|lt=Mount Kasuga|1=Kasugayama}} for imperial ice offerings. After the capital moved to {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|qid=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}}, the {{ill|Himuro Shrine|en|Himuro Shrine|qid=Q11549570|lt=Himuro Shrine|1=Himuro Shrine}} was established in {{ill|Kasugano, Nara City|ja|春日野町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasugano, Nara City|1=Kasugano, Nara City|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q18988212}}, enshrining the Himuro from Nara.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Himuro Shrine|url=http://www.himurojinja.jp|publisher=Himuro Shrine Official Website|accessdate=2012-05-21}}</ref> It is also known as {{ill|Himuro Gongen|ja|氷室権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himuro Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 氷室}}.
=== Place Names ===
Himuro often remains as a place name where it once existed. Examples include "Himuro-cho" in {{ill|Nishigamo, Kyoto|ja|西賀茂氷室町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishigamo, Kyoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Kyoto, and "Himuro" in {{ill|Kamikawa, Izumo City|ja|神氷氷室|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamikawa, Izumo City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|qid=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture|1=Shimane Prefecture}}. There are also numerous settlements across Japan named {{ill|Himuro Village|ja|氷室村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himuro Village|1=Himuro Village|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11549564}} and {{ill|Himuro Town|ja|氷室町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himuro Town|1=himurochou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q18989720}}.
Additionally, names like {{ill|Mount Himuro|ja|氷室山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Himuro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135578952|1=Mount Himuro}}, {{ill|Mount Himuro-dake|ja|氷室岳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Himuro-dake|1=Mount Himuro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11549563}}, Himuro Plateau, Himuro River, Himuro Valley, {{ill|Himuro Falls|ja|氷室大滝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himuro Falls|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135578954}}, Himuro Pond, and Himuro Wakare (an intersection or junction) can also be found.
== Traditional Events ==
On the last Sunday of {{ill|January|en|January|qid=Q108|lt=January|1=January}}, snow is packed into the Himuro hut (氷室の仕込み), and on {{ill|June 30|en|June 30|qid=Q2657|lt=June 30|1=June 30}}, the snow is taken out, an event called "Himuro Opening" (氷室開き). In the heavy snowfall region of {{ill|Ishikawa Prefecture|en|Ishikawa Prefecture|qid=Q131281|lt=Ishikawa Prefecture|1=Ishikawa Prefecture}} and {{ill|Kanazawa|en|Kanazawa|qid=Q191130|lt=Kanazawa City|1=Kanazawa}} and its surroundings, there used to be many Himuro sites. Ice was a valuable commodity for common people during the summer, and a custom emerged to eat {{ill|Himuro Manju|ja|氷室饅頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himuro Manju|1=Himuro Manju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11549574}}, said to resemble ice, on {{ill|July 1|en|July 1|qid=Q2700|lt=July 1|1=July 1}} to pray for {{ill|Health|en|Health|qid=Q12147|lt=health|1=Health}}, a tradition that continues today. The Himuro Opening event ceased in the {{ill|1950s|ja|昭和30th year 代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1950s|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (around {{ill|1955|en|1955|qid=Q18577|lt=1955|1=1955}}-{{ill|1965|en|1965|qid=Q2650|lt=1965|1=1965}}) but was revived in {{ill|1986|en|1986|qid=Q2430|lt=1986|1=1986}} (Showa 61). However, in recent years, continuous warm winters have led to snow shortages, and the event was once threatened when the {{ill|Hakuenro Hotel|ja|白雲楼ホテル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakuenro Hotel|1=Hakuenro Hotel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11580438}}, which owned the Himuro hut at {{ill|Yuwaku Onsen|en|Yuwaku Onsen|qid=Q11563731|lt=Yuwaku Onsen|1=Yuwaku Onsen}}, went bankrupt in {{ill|1998|en|1998|qid=Q2089|lt=1998|1=1998}} (Heisei 10). Thanks to the permission of the bankruptcy trustee, the Himuro event near {{ill|Gyokusen Lake|ja|玉泉湖|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyokusen Lake|1=Lake Gyokusen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11572696}} continues today, with ice presented to local areas (Ishikawa Prefecture and Kanazawa City) as well as {{ill|Itabashi|en|Itabashi|qid=Q232635|lt=Itabashi Ward|1=Itabashi}} and {{ill|Meguro|en|Meguro|qid=Q233903|lt=Meguro Ward|1=Meguro}} in Tokyo, which have historical ties to the {{ill|Maeda clan|en|Maeda clan|qid=Q578033|lt=Maeda clan|1=Maeda clan}}.<ref>[http://yuwaku.gr.jp/himuro/ Himuro] Yuwaku Tourism Association (Accessed July 10, 2018)</ref><ref>[https://www.city.meguro.tokyo.jp/smph/gyosei/koho/hodo_news/20180704.html "Himuro Snow and Ice" Arrives at Meguro Ward Office - A Cool Gift from Kanazawa Yuwaku Onsen (July 4, 2018)] Meguro Ward (Accessed July 10, 2018)</ref>
In {{ill|Yatsushiro|en|Yatsushiro|qid=Q734479|lt=Yatsushiro City|1=Yatsushiro}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|qid=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture|1=Kumamoto Prefecture}}, the {{ill|Himuro Festival|ja|氷室祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himuro Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135578957|1=Himuro Festival}} is held at {{ill|Yatsushiro Shrine|ja|八代神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsushiro Shrine|qq=}} (also known as {{ill|Myoken Shrine|ja|妙見宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoken Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 妙見宮 (曖昧さ回避)|1=Myōken Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q16767999}}) from {{ill|May 31|en|May 31|qid=Q2591|lt=May 31|1=May 31}} to {{ill|June 1|en|June 1|qid=Q2625|lt=June 1|1=June 1}}.<ref name="KumamotoPublicRelations">{{Cite web|title=Himuro Festival Specialty Yukimochi - Kumamoto's Culinary Traditions |url=http://www.kininaru-k.jp/2008/back_doc/05/0529/syoku.html |work=Kumamoto Public Relations (Official Website) |publisher=Kumamoto Prefecture PR Office, Colors Planning Co., Ltd. |accessdate=2012-05-21}}</ref> This festival day is colloquially known as "Kootsuitachi" (氷朔日), and it is customary to eat {{ill|Yukimochi|ja|雪餅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yukimochi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, rice cakes made by kneading {{ill|Rice flour|en|Rice flour|qid=Q1269205|lt=rice flour|1=Rice flour}} with water and steaming them in a {{ill|Bamboo steamer|en|Bamboo steamer|qid=Q3267901|lt=seiro (steamer)|1=Bamboo steamer}}, resembling snow clumps.<ref name="KumamotoPublicRelations" />
In {{ill|Nikkō|en|Nikkō|qid=Q235753|lt=Nikkō City|1=Nikkō}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|en|Tochigi Prefecture|qid=Q44843|lt=Tochigi Prefecture|1=Tochigi Prefecture}}, there used to be about ten natural ice producers. Today, only three remain:<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.shimotsuke.co.jp/dosoon/official/20120105/693564|title=Shimotsuke Newspaper Video News DoSOON - Natural Ice Harvest Begins in Nikko|accessdate=2012-08-25}}</ref> the fourth-generation {{ill|Ice Maker Tokujirō|ja|氷屋徳次郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ice Maker Tokujirō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Yoshishin Himuro|ja|吉新氷室|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshishin Himuro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135578959|1=Yoshishin Himuro}})<ref>[http://www.chirorin.com/buy_ice.html Natural Ice | Official Website of Chirorin Village, Nikko Kirifuri Highlands]</ref><ref>[http://foodpianikko.jp/SHOP/nni10.html Nikko Natural Ice by the Fourth Generation Tokujirō]</ref>, {{ill|Shogetsu Himuro|ja|松月氷室|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shogetsu Himuro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135578962|1=Shogetsu Himuro}}<ref>[https://ameblo.jp/shogetsu-goodjob/entry-11059313541.html A Detailed Story About Natural Ice]</ref><ref>[https://ameblo.jp/shogetsu-goodjob/entry-10926137751.html Do You Know About "Natural Ice"?]</ref>, and {{ill|Mitsuboshi Himuro|ja|三ツ星氷室|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuboshi Himuro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135578965|1=Mitsuboshi Himuro}}, all of which store and sell natural ice.
==See Also==
